title: Introduction to type
text: “type” {type} defines set restrictions used validate objects R. example, type t_bool (“boolean type”) requires object: bare logical vector. scalar (size 1). Contains NA values. words, object type t_bool must either TRUE FALSE. type printed, displays requirements object type must meet: obj_is_type() may used test whether object meets requirements given type. additional information type test failed succeeded, use obj_inspect_type() print diagnostic message. variable may restricted given type using %:% operator: variable typed, may re-assigned variable type, assigned different type: Alternatively, obj_assert_type() may used interactively enforce type object: %:%, obj_assert_type(), type validation functions {type} raise {rlang} errors class <type_error_mistyped> object mistyped.
code: print(t_bool) #> <type> #> • `<object>` is a bare <logical>. #> • `<object>` is size 1. #> • `<object>` contains no missing values. obj_is_type(10L, t_bool) #> [1] FALSE obj_is_type(TRUE, t_bool) #> [1] TRUE obj_inspect_type(NA, t_bool) #> Object `NA` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `NA` is a bare <logical>. #> ✔ `NA` is size 1. #> ℹ `NA` must not contain missing elements. #> ✖ `NA` is NA at location `1`. # Require that `x` is an integer, and initialize `x` to `1:5` t_int %:% x(1:5) x  #> [1] 1 2 3 4 5 x <- 2L x #> [1] 2  x <- "A" #> Error: #> ! Attempted to assign a mistyped value to `x`. #> ✖ `<value>` must be a bare <integer>, not a bare <character>. #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type. obj_assert_type(data.frame(x = 1:3), t_int) #> Error in `obj_assert_type()`: #> ! Object `data.frame(x = 1:3)` is mistyped. #> ℹ `data.frame(x = 1:3)` must be a bare <integer>. #> ✖ `data.frame(x = 1:3)` is a S3 object of class <data.frame>. #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type.

title: Introduction to type
text: {type} includes following base types common R objects:

title: Introduction to type
text: Every type built using traits, add restrictions type. example, base t_string type built using sized() complete() traits: Traits compose naturally using |> operator, making easy define custom types.
code: # A string is: t_string <- t_chr |> # A character vector   sized(1L) |>       # Of size 1   complete()         # Which is non-missing t_probability <- t_dbl |> bounded(0, 1, "[]")  obj_inspect_type(0.5, t_probability) #> Object `0.5` has the expected type. #> ✔ `0.5` is a bare <double>. #> ✔ `0.5` is bounded by [0, 1].

title: Introduction to type
text: {type} defines small dialect setting type constraints parts object using () selector functions, (), has_relation(): useful building complex types simple set traits. list_type() dataframe_type() provide shorthand typed containers: list_of_type() constructs common case homogenous list type:
code: # `names()` of an object must contain "x" and "y" t_any |> has(on(names), t_chr |> contains(c("x", "y"))) #> <type> #> • `names(<object>)` is a bare <character>. #> • `names(<object>)` contains elements: `c("x", "y")`.  # Element `[[1]]` of an object must be a function t_any |> has(on_elm(1L), t_fun) #> <type> #> • `<object>[[1]]` is a function.  # Every element of the object must be the same size t_list |> has_relation(same_sized(on_each())) #> <type> #> • `<object>` is a bare <list>. #> • Each element of `<object>` are the same size. t_coordinates <- t_list |>   has(on(names), t_chr |> same_as(c("lat", "lon"))) |>   has(on_elm("lat"), t_dbl |> bounded(-90, 90)) |>   has(on_elm("lon"), t_dbl |> bounded(-180, 180)) |>   has_relation(same_sized(on_elm("lat"), on_elm("lon")))   good <- list(lat = c(51.5, 40.7), lon = c(-0.1, -74.0)) bad <- list(lat = c(51.5, 200.0), lon = c(-0.1, -74.0, 0.0)) worst <- list(x = "A")   obj_is_type(good, t_coordinates) #> [1] TRUE obj_inspect_type(bad, t_coordinates) #> Object `bad` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `bad` is a bare <list>. #> ✔ `names(bad)` is a bare <character>. #> ✔ `names(bad)` is the same as: `c("lat", "lon")`. #> ℹ `bad[["lat"]]` must be bounded by [-90, 90]. #> ✖ `bad[["lat"]]` is out of bounds at location `2`. obj_inspect_type(worst, t_coordinates) #> Object `worst` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `worst` is a bare <list>. #> ℹ `names(worst)` must be: `c("lat", "lon")`. #> ✖ `names(worst)` is missing 2 elements: `c("lat", "lon")`. #> ✖ `names(worst)` contains 1 unexpected element: `"x"`. t_survey <- dataframe_type(   id = t_int |> complete(),    score = t_dbl |> bounded(0, 100) ) print(t_survey) #> <type> #> • `<object>` inherits from class `data.frame`. #> • `names(<object>)` is a bare <character>. #> • `names(<object>)` is the same as: `c("id", "score")`. #> • `<object>[["id"]]` is a bare <integer>. #> • `<object>[["id"]]` contains no missing values. #> • `<object>[["score"]]` is a bare <double>. #> • `<object>[["score"]]` is bounded by [0, 100]. t_list_of_chr <- list_of_type(t_chr)  obj_is_type(list("A", "B", "C"), t_list_of_chr) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(list("A", 2L, "C"), t_list_of_chr) #> [1] FALSE

title: Introduction to type
text: variable may declared constant using const() modifier, case ’s value changed assignment.
code: const(t_int) %:% z(1L) z <- 2L #> Error: #> ! Can't assign to the constant `z`. #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type.

title: Introduction to type
text: Two types may combined type union, requires object satisfy least one types’ constraints. Failed type union checks report unmet requirement type union:
code: t_rownames <- type_union(t_int, t_chr) |> complete()  obj_is_type(FALSE, t_rownames) #> [1] FALSE obj_is_type(1:3, t_rownames) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(c("a", "b", "c"), t_rownames) #> [1] TRUE obj_inspect_type(NA_integer_, t_rownames) #> Object `NA_integer_` does not have the expected type. #> • Type option 1 of 2: #> ✔ `NA_integer_` is a bare <integer>. #> ℹ `NA_integer_` must not contain missing elements. #> ✖ `NA_integer_` is NA at location `1`. #> • Type option 2 of 2: #> ✖ `NA_integer_` must be a bare <character>, not a bare <integer>.

title: Introduction to type
text: typed function declared using typed(). Arguments without default values annotated using type (e.g. x = t_chr) default values provided using %:% operator. called, typed function validates typed arguments, raising error argument mistyped. last_type(), interactively debugging failed argument object type checks simple. return type function may specified using returns argument. Additionally, relation compatible has_relation() may also used define -argument constraints. , require x, start, stop arguments safe_substr() either length-1 length, using recyclable() relation: Type checks may removed typed function using untyped(), useful bypassing argument validation pre-validated performance sensitive areas code.
code: str_remove <- typed(function(   x = t_chr,    pattern = t_string,    fixed = t_bool %:% FALSE ) {   base::gsub(x = x, pattern = pattern, replacement = "", fixed = fixed) }) print(str_remove) #> <typed> #> function (x, pattern, fixed = FALSE)  #> { #>     base::gsub(x = x, pattern = pattern, replacement = "", fixed = fixed) #> } #> Arguments: #> • `x` is a bare <character>. #> • `pattern` is a bare <character>. #> • `pattern` is size 1. #> • `pattern` contains no missing values. #> • `fixed` is a bare <logical>. #> • `fixed` is size 1. #> • `fixed` contains no missing values. #> Returns: #> • `<result>` is an R object. str_remove("hello", pattern = "he") #> [1] "llo" str_remove("goodbye", pattern = NA_character_) #> Error in `str_remove()`: #> ! Argument `pattern` is mistyped. #> ℹ `pattern` must not contain missing elements. #> ✖ `pattern` is NA at location `1`. #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type. # What type was expected? t_expected <- last_type() print(t_expected) #> <type> #> • `<object>` is a bare <character>. #> • `<object>` is size 1. #> • `<object>` contains no missing values.  # How does my input compare? pattern <- NA_character_ obj_inspect_type(pattern, t_expected) #> Object `pattern` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `pattern` is a bare <character>. #> ✔ `pattern` is size 1. #> ℹ `pattern` must not contain missing elements. #> ✖ `pattern` is NA at location `1`. safe_any <- typed(   function(... = t_lgl, na.rm = t_bool %:% FALSE) {     base::any(..., na.rm = na.rm)   },   returns = t_lgl |> sized(1L) ) print(safe_any) #> <typed> #> function (..., na.rm = FALSE)  #> { #>     base::any(..., na.rm = na.rm) #> } #> Arguments: #> • Each element of `...` is a bare <logical>. #> • `na.rm` is a bare <logical>. #> • `na.rm` is size 1. #> • `na.rm` contains no missing values. #> Returns: #> • `<result>` is a bare <logical>. #> • `<result>` is size 1. safe_substr <- typed(   recyclable(x, start, stop),   function(x = t_chr, start = t_int, stop = t_int) {     base::substr(x, start, stop)   },   returns = t_chr )  safe_substr(c("works", "fine"), 1L, 2L) #> [1] "wo" "fi" safe_substr(c("this", "wont", "work"), 1:2, 1:2) #> Error in `safe_substr()`: #> ! Arguments `x`, `start`, and `stop` must be recyclable. #> ✖ `x` (size 3) and `start` (size 2) are incompatible sizes. untyped(safe_any) #> function (..., na.rm = FALSE)  #> { #>     base::any(..., na.rm = na.rm) #> }

title: Authors and Citation
text: Ethan Sansom. Author, maintainer, copyright holder.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Sansom E (2026). type: Runtime Type Validation Functions Objects. R package version 0.0.0.9000, https://github.com/EthanSansom/type.
code: @Manual{,   title = {type: Runtime Type Validation for Functions and Objects},   author = {Ethan Sansom},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000},   url = {https://github.com/EthanSansom/type}, }

what: Runtime Type Validation for Functions and Objects
title: Runtime Type Validation for Functions and Objects
text: {type} provides lightweight type system R, meant users quickly easily add run-time type validation functions variables. {type} package supports: Adding type annotations function arguments return values. Declaring typed variables constants. Defining new types type aliases.

title: Runtime Type Validation for Functions and Objects
text: can install development version {type} GitHub :
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("EthanSansom/type")

title: Runtime Type Validation for Functions and Objects
text: {type} provides collection built-types, t_int (integer) t_bool (boolean), may used annotate objects, function arguments, return values. variable’s type may declared using %:% operator, constrains values may assigned . Every type {type} composed traits, functions add additional restrictions type. example, t_string type built sized() complete() traits: type object can checked using obj_is_type(), obj_inspect_type(), obj_assert_type(): support complex type definitions, {type} provides small dialect setting type constraints parts object using () selector functions, (), has_relation(): useful building complex types simple set traits.
code: t_int %:% x(10L) # Assigning another integer to `x` is okay x <- 1:3  # But non-integer assignment raises an error x <- "A" #> Error: #> ! Attempted to assign a mistyped value to `x`. #> ✖ `<value>` must be a bare <integer>, not a bare <character>. #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type. # A string is: t_string <- t_chr |> # A character vector   sized(1L) |>       # Of size 1   complete()         # Which is non-missing # Returns TRUE or FALSE obj_is_type(NA_character_, t_string) #> [1] FALSE  # Prints a diagnostic message obj_inspect_type(NA_character_, t_string) #> Object `NA_character_` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `NA_character_` is a bare <character>. #> ✔ `NA_character_` is size 1. #> ℹ `NA_character_` must not contain missing elements. #> ✖ `NA_character_` is NA at location `1`.  # Raises an error (on failure) or returns `NULL` (on success) obj_assert_type(NA_character_, t_string) #> Error in `obj_assert_type()`: #> ! Object `NA_character_` is mistyped. #> ℹ `NA_character_` must not contain missing elements. #> ✖ `NA_character_` is NA at location `1`. #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type. # `names()` of an object must contain "x" and "y" t_any |> has(on(names), t_chr |> contains(c("x", "y"))) #> <type> #> • `names(<object>)` is a bare <character>. #> • `names(<object>)` contains elements: `c("x", "y")`.  # Element `[[1]]` of an object must be a function t_any |> has(on_elm(1L), t_fun) #> <type> #> • `<object>[[1]]` is a function.  # Every element of the object must be the same size t_list |> has_relation(same_sized(on_each())) #> <type> #> • `<object>` is a bare <list>. #> • Each element of `<object>` are the same size. t_point <- t_list |>   has(on(names), t_chr |> setequal_to(c("x", "y"))) |>   has(on_elm("x"), t_int |> complete()) |>   has(on_elm("y"), t_int |> complete()) |>   has_relation(same_sized(on_elms(c("x", "y"))))   good <- list(x = 1:2, y = 3:4) bad <- list(x = c(1L, NA), y = c(0L, 0L))   obj_is_type(good, t_point) #> [1] TRUE obj_inspect_type(bad, t_point) #> Object `bad` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `bad` is a bare <list>. #> ✔ `names(bad)` is a bare <character>. #> ✔ `names(bad)` is setequal to: `c("x", "y")`. #> ℹ `bad[["x"]]` must not contain missing elements. #> ✖ `bad[["x"]]` is NA at location `2`.

title: Runtime Type Validation for Functions and Objects
text: typed function declared using typed(): Arguments without default values annotated using type (e.g. x = t_num) default values provided using %:% operator. called, typed function checks inputs correct type raises error otherwise. addition argument types, return type function may also specified using returns argument. Typed functions also support -argument constaints, using trait compatible has_relation(). example, na_string() requires arguments x na_to either size length-1, using recyclable() trait.
code: safe_log <- typed(function(x = t_num, base = t_num %:% exp(1)) {    base::log(x, base = base)  }) print(safe_log) #> <typed> #> function (x, base = exp(1))  #> { #>     base::log(x, base = base) #> } #> <environment: 0x107a4a518> #> Arguments: #> • `x` is a numeric vector. #> • `base` is a numeric vector. #> Returns: #> • `<result>` is an R object. safe_log(10) #> [1] 2.302585 safe_log("A") #> Error in `safe_log()`: #> ! Argument `x` is mistyped. #> ✖ `x` must be a numeric vector, not the string "A". #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type. safe_any <- typed(   function(... = t_lgl, na.rm = t_bool %:% FALSE) {     base::any(..., na.rm = na.rm)   },   returns = t_lgl |> sized(1L) ) print(safe_any) #> <typed> #> function (..., na.rm = FALSE)  #> { #>     base::any(..., na.rm = na.rm) #> } #> <environment: 0x107a4a518> #> Arguments: #> • Each element of `...` is a bare <logical>. #> • `na.rm` is a bare <logical>. #> • `na.rm` is size 1. #> • `na.rm` contains no missing values. #> Returns: #> • `<result>` is a bare <logical>. #> • `<result>` is size 1. na_string <- typed(   recyclable(x, na_to),   function(x = t_chr, na_to = t_chr |> complete()) {     x[is.na(x)] <- na_to     x   },   returns = t_chr |> complete() )  na_string(c("hi", NA, "bye"), na_to = "N/A") #> [1] "hi"  "N/A" "bye" na_string(c("hi", NA), na_to = character()) #> Error in `na_string()`: #> ! Arguments `x` and `na_to` must be recyclable. #> ✖ `x` (size 2) and `na_to` (size 0) are incompatible sizes.

title: Check typeof() — bare_typed
text: bare_typed() returns copy type requires objects bare R value typeof() equal typeof. Bare objects class attribute, example 1L list(), data.frame().
code: t_lgl <- t_any |> bare_typed("logical") obj_is_type(1, t_lgl)  # FALSE obj_is_type(NA, t_lgl) # TRUE

title: Check typeof() — bare_typed
code: bare_typed(type, typeof)

title: Check typeof() — bare_typed
text: type type. typeof string. Must one valid R types returned typeof(): "double", "integer", "logical", "character", "complex", "raw", "list", "NULL", "environment", "symbol", "pairlist", "language", "expression", "S4", "closure", "special", "builtin", "externalptr", "weakref", "promise", "char", "bytecode".

title: Check typeof() — bare_typed
text: copy type additional typeof constraint.

title: Check typeof() — bare_typed
code: t_pairlist <- t_any |> bare_typed("pairlist") obj_inspect_type(10L, t_pairlist) #> Object `10L` does not have the expected type. #> ✖ `10L` must be a bare <pairlist>, not a bare <integer>. obj_inspect_type(pairlist(), t_pairlist) #> Object `pairlist()` has the expected type. #> ✔ `pairlist()` is a bare <pairlist>.

title: Built-in types — base-types
text: t_* types represent common objects R, functions atomic vectors. may refined using traits, complete() bounded(), add additional restrictions type. t_* type S7 object containing list traits. t_bool example constructed bare_typed(), sized(), complete():   following base types provided:
code: t_bool <- bare_typed("logical") |> sized(1L) |> complete()

title: Built-in types — base-types
code: t_any  t_null  t_list  t_env  t_fun  t_vec  t_num  t_lgl  t_bool  t_int  t_dbl  t_chr  t_string  t_dataframe  t_factor  t_date  t_posixct  t_dots

title: Built-in types — base-types
code: obj_is_type(1L, t_int) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(1.5, t_int) #> [1] FALSE  obj_is_type(TRUE, t_bool) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(NA, t_bool) #> [1] FALSE obj_is_type(c(TRUE, FALSE), t_bool) #> [1] FALSE  obj_is_type("hello", t_string) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(NA_character_, t_string) #> [1] FALSE  # Add traits to enforce additional restrictions t_prob <- t_dbl |> bounded(0, 1) t_name <- t_string |> within(c("x", "y", "z"))

what: Check upper and lower bounds — bounded
title: Check upper and lower bounds — bounded
text: bounded() returns copy type requires objects fall within range. Bounds specified left right, interval type controlled bounds: Either left right may omitted leave side unbounded:   bounded() ignores NA values checking bounds. require non-missingness, combine complete() trait. [)"  | Right-open   |left <= x & x < right | |"(]: R:)%22%60%20%20%20%7C%20Right-open%20%20%20%7C%20%60left%20%3C=%20x%20&%20x%20%3C%20right%60%20%20%7C%0A%7C%20%60%22( [0, 1]: R:0,%201
code: t_dbl |> bounded(0, 1)             # probabilities: [0, 1] t_dbl |> bounded(0, bounds = "()") # strictly positive t_chr |> bounded("m", "p")         # starts with "m", "n", "o", or "p"

title: Check upper and lower bounds — bounded
code: bounded(type, left = NULL, right = NULL, bounds = "[]")

title: Check upper and lower bounds — bounded
text: type type. left, right Scalar values giving left right bounds. Either may NULL (default) leave side unbounded. least one left, right must supplied. bounds string denoting boundary type: "[]" (closed, default), "[)", "(]", "()" (open). [)", "(]: R:)%22%60,%20%60%22(

title: Check upper and lower bounds — bounded
text: copy type additional bound constraint.

title: Check upper and lower bounds — bounded
code: t_prob <- t_dbl |> bounded(0, 1) t_positive <- t_dbl |> bounded(0, bounds = "()")  obj_inspect_type(0.5, t_prob) #> Object `0.5` has the expected type. #> ✔ `0.5` is a bare <double>. #> ✔ `0.5` is bounded by [0, 1]. obj_inspect_type(0, t_positive) #> Object `0` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `0` is a bare <double>. #> ℹ `0` must be above 0. #> ✖ `0` is out of bounds at location `1`.  # `NA` values are considered within bounds obj_is_type(c(0, NA, 0.75), t_prob) #> [1] TRUE

title: Check class() — classed
text: classed() returns copy type requires objects inherit one classes. inherits argument controls whether object must inherit supplied classes least one:
code: # Must inherit from "Date" t_date <- t_any |> classed("Date")  # Must inherit from both "POSIXct" and "POSIXt" t_posixct <- t_any |> classed(c("POSIXct", "POSIXt"), inherits = "all")  # Must inherit from "Date" or "POSIXct" t_datetime <- t_any |> classed(c("Date", "POSIXct"), inherits = "any")

title: Check class() — classed
code: classed(type, classes, inherits = "all")

title: Check class() — classed
text: type type. classes character vector class names. inherits Whether object must inherit "" supplied classes least "" one. Defaults "".

title: Check class() — classed
text: copy type additional class constraint.

title: Check class() — classed
code: t_posixct <- t_any |> classed(c("POSIXct", "POSIXt"), inherits = "all") obj_is_type(as.POSIXct("2020-01-01"), t_posixct) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(as.Date("2020-01-01"), t_posixct) #> [1] FALSE

title: Check for missing values — complete
text: complete() returns copy type requires objects contain missing (.e. NA) values, checked via vctrs::vec_any_missing().

title: Check for missing values — complete
code: complete(type)

title: Check for missing values — complete
text: type type.

title: Check for missing values — complete
text: copy type additional non-missingness constraint.

title: Check for missing values — complete
code: t_real <- t_dbl |> complete() obj_inspect_type(10.6, t_real) #> Object `10.6` has the expected type. #> ✔ `10.6` is a bare <double>. #> ✔ `10.6` contains no missing values. obj_inspect_type(c(1, NA), t_real) #> Object `c(1, NA)` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `c(1, NA)` is a bare <double>. #> ℹ `c(1, NA)` must not contain missing elements. #> ✖ `c(1, NA)` is NA at location `2`.

what: Check that object is a superset — contains
title: Check that object is a superset — contains
text: contains() returns copy type requires objects contain elements values, checked via vctrs::vec_in().

title: Check that object is a superset — contains
code: contains(type, values)

title: Check that object is a superset — contains
text: type type. values non-empty, non-list vector values objects must contain.

title: Check that object is a superset — contains
text: copy type additional constraint objects contain elements values.

title: Check that object is a superset — contains
code: t_rgb <- t_chr |> contains(c("r", "g", "b")) obj_inspect_type(c("r", "g", "b", "a"), t_rgb) #> Object `c("r", "g", "b", "a")` has the expected type. #> ✔ `c("r", "g", "b", "a")` is a bare <character>. #> ✔ `c("r", "g", "b", "a")` contains elements: `c("r", "g", "b")`. obj_inspect_type(c("r", "g"), t_rgb) #> Object `c("r", "g")` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `c("r", "g")` is a bare <character>. #> ℹ `c("r", "g")` must contain elements: `c("r", "g", "b")`. #> ✖ `c("r", "g")` is missing 1 element: `"b"`.

what: Check that object does not contain values — disjoint_to
title: Check that object does not contain values — disjoint_to
text: disjoint_to() returns copy type requires object contain values, checked via vctrs::vec_in().

title: Check that object does not contain values — disjoint_to
code: disjoint_to(type, values)

title: Check that object does not contain values — disjoint_to
text: type type. values non-empty, non-list vector values object must contain.

title: Check that object does not contain values — disjoint_to
text: copy type additional constraint objects contain none values.

title: Check that object does not contain values — disjoint_to
code: t_not_three <- t_int |> setequal_to(1:3) obj_inspect_type(10L, t_not_three) #> Object `10L` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `10L` is a bare <integer>. #> ℹ `10L` must contain exactly: `c(1, 2, 3)`. #> ✖ `10L` is missing 3 elements: `c(1, 2, 3)`. #> ✖ `10L` contains 1 unexpected element: `10`. obj_inspect_type(c(1L, 2L, 3L, 1L), t_not_three) #> Object `c(1L, 2L, 3L, 1L)` has the expected type. #> ✔ `c(1L, 2L, 3L, 1L)` is a bare <integer>. #> ✔ `c(1L, 2L, 3L, 1L)` is setequal to: `c(1, 2, 3)`.

title: Declare a typed object — %:%
text: %:% declares typed variable calling environment. right-hand side must declaration form name(value), name variable create value initial value:   typed variable behaves like regular object, type checked every assignment. constant variable may declared using const(), case variable modified.
code: t_int %:% x(1L) x        # 1L x <- 2L  # ok x <- "A" # error, "A" is not an integer const(t_int) %:% x(1L) x <- 2L # error, can't assign to a constant

title: Declare a typed object — %:%
code: type %:% declaration

title: Declare a typed object — %:%
text: type type, optionally wrapped const(). declaration declaration form name(value). name becomes variable name value used variable's initial value.

title: Declare a typed object — %:%
text: initial value, invisibly.

title: Declare a typed object — %:%
code: t_chr |> sized(1L) %:% x("hello") const(t_bool) %:% y(TRUE)  # `x` must be a scalar character x <- "goodbye" try(x <- 10) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : Attempted to assign a mistyped value to `y`. #> ✖ `<value>` must be a bare <character>, not a bare <double>. #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type.  # `y` can't be assigned to try(y <- FALSE) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : Can't assign to the constant `y`. #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type.  # Typed variables can be re-declared as a different type t_chr %:% y("true")

what: Add an element or attribute constraint to a type — has
title: Add an element or attribute constraint to a type — has
text: () returns copy type requires selected part object type on_type. part identified selector (see ()).   Constraints can composed pipe operator |>:
code: # Require that element `[[1]]` is an integer t_any |> has(on_elm(1L), t_int)  # Require that the "dim" attribute is an integer vector of size 2 t_any |> has(on_attr("dim"), t_int |> sized(2L))  # Require that names contains "x" t_any |> has(on(names), t_chr |> contains("x")) t_coords <- t_list |>   has(on_elm("lat"), t_dbl |> bounded(-90, 90)) |>   has(on_elm("lon"), t_dbl |> bounded(-180, 180))

title: Add an element or attribute constraint to a type — has
code: has(type, selector, on_type)

title: Add an element or attribute constraint to a type — has
text: type type. selector selector, e.g. result (), on_elm(), on_attr(). on_type type check selected value .

title: Add an element or attribute constraint to a type — has
text: copy type additional element attributeconstraint.

title: Add an element or attribute constraint to a type — has
code: # Constrain a list element by position or name t_pair <- t_list |>   has(on_elm(1L), t_chr) |>   has(on_elm("a"), t_int)  obj_is_type(list("a", a = 1L), t_pair) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(list("a", a = 1.5), t_pair) #> [1] FALSE  # on() accepts any accessor call, using .x as a placeholder t_short <- t_chr |> has(on(length(.x)), t_int |> bounded(1L, 5L))  obj_is_type(c("a", "b"), t_short) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(character(), t_short) #> [1] FALSE obj_is_type(letters, t_short) #> [1] FALSE  # on() also accepts a bare function name as shorthand for f(.x) t_dict <- t_list |> has(on(names), t_chr |> unduplicated())  obj_is_type(list(x = 1, y = 2), t_dict) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(list(x = 1, x = 2), t_dict) #> [1] FALSE  # on_each() checks the type of every element t_list_of_dbl <- t_list |> has(on_each(), t_dbl)  obj_is_type(list(1.1, 2.2, 3.3), t_list_of_dbl) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(list(1.1, 2L, 3.3), t_list_of_dbl) #> [1] FALSE

what: Add a between-element constraint to a type — has_relation
title: Add a between-element constraint to a type — has_relation
text: has_relation() returns copy type requires relationship hold selected parts object. Parts selected using selector functions (see ) relationship expressed relation (see same_sized(), same_classed(), exclusive()).   () has_relation() can composed:
code: # Require that elements `[[1]]` and `[[2]]` are the same size t_any |> has_relation(same_sized(on_elm(1L), on_elm(2L)))  # Require that attributes "x" and "y" have the same class t_any |> has_relation(same_classed(on_attr("x"), on_attr("y")))  # Require that only one of attributes "x" and "y" are non-NULL t_any |> has_relation(exclusive(on_attrs(c("x", "y"))) t_coords <- t_list |>   has(on_elm("lat"), t_dbl |> bounded(-90, 90)) |>   has(on_elm("lon"), t_dbl |> bounded(-180, 180)) |>   has_relation(same_sized(on_elm("lat"), on_elm("lon")))

title: Add a between-element constraint to a type — has_relation
code: has_relation(type, relation)

title: Add a between-element constraint to a type — has_relation
text: type type. relation relation, e.g. result same_sized(), same_classed(), exclusive().

title: Add a between-element constraint to a type — has_relation
text: copy type additional -element constraint.

title: Add a between-element constraint to a type — has_relation
code: # Require that elements `[[1]]` and `[[2]]` are the same size t <- t_any |> has_relation(same_sized(on_elm(1L), on_elm(2L))) obj_inspect_type(list(1:3, 1:3), t) #> Object `list(1:3, 1:3)` has the expected type. #> ✔ `list(1:3, 1:3)[[1]]` and `list(1:3, 1:3)[[2]]` are the same size. obj_inspect_type(list(1:3, 1:2), t) #> Object `list(1:3, 1:2)` does not have the expected type. #> ℹ `list(1:3, 1:2)[[1]]` and `list(1:3, 1:2)[[2]]` must be the same size. #> ✖ `list(1:3, 1:2)[[1]]` is size 3 and `list(1:3, 1:2)[[2]]` is size 2.  # Require that attributes "x" and "y" have the same class t <- t_any |> has_relation(same_classed(on_attr("x"), on_attr("y"))) good <- structure(list(), x = 1L, y = 2L) bad <- structure(list(), x = 1L, y = "a") obj_inspect_type(good, t) #> Object `good` has the expected type. #> ✔ `attr(good, "x")` and `attr(good, "y")` have the same class. obj_inspect_type(bad, t) #> Object `bad` does not have the expected type. #> ℹ `attr(bad, "x")` and `attr(bad, "y")` must have the same class. #> ✖ `attr(bad, "x")` has class <integer> and `attr(bad, "y")` has class #>   <character>.

title: Inlined helper functions — inline_assert_same_classed
text: functions inserted typed() functions meant external use. exported ensure typed() functions call correct helper, e.g. type::inline_assert_type(), globally defined function, e.g. inline_assert_type().

title: Inlined helper functions — inline_assert_same_classed
code: inline_assert_same_classed(..., error_call = rlang::caller_env())  inline_assert_same_sized(..., error_call = rlang::caller_env())  inline_assert_recyclable(..., error_call = rlang::caller_env())  inline_assert_exclusive(..., error_call = rlang::caller_env())  inline_abort_mistyped(   type,   message,   error_subclass = character(),   error_call = rlang::caller_env() )  inline_obj_type_validate(obj, obj_name, type)  inline_assert_type(   obj,   obj_name,   type,   error_header,   error_subclass = character(),   error_call = rlang::caller_env() )  inline_arg_assert_type(arg, type, arg_name, error_call = rlang::caller_env())  inline_dots_assert_type(..., .type, .error_call = rlang::caller_env())  inline_dotlist_assert_type(dots, type, error_call = rlang::caller_env())

title: Inlined helper functions — inline_assert_same_classed
text: ... Dots checked individually. error_call, .error_call call use error messages. type, .type type. message error message. error_subclass error class. obj object checked. obj_name object name use error messages. error_header error message header. arg argument checked. arg_name argument name use error messages. dots list dots checked.

title: Inlined helper functions — inline_assert_same_classed
code: try(inline_dots_assert_type(10L, t_chr)) #> Error in inline_dots_assert_type(10L, t_chr) :  #>   argument ".type" is missing, with no default

what: Return the expected type of the last mistyped object — last_type
title: Return the expected type of the last mistyped object — last_type
text: last_type() returns expected type last object fail type check obj_assert_type() typed().

title: Return the expected type of the last mistyped object — last_type
code: last_type()

title: Return the expected type of the last mistyped object — last_type
text: last expected type. type assertions run, returns NULL.

title: Return the expected type of the last mistyped object — last_type
code: # Returns `NULL` if no type checks have been run last_type() #> <type> #> • `<object>` is a bare <logical>. #> • `<object>` is size 1. #> • `<object>` contains no missing values.  # `last_type()` returns `t_bool` after failed assertion if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ obj_assert_type(10L, t_bool) last_type() } # }

title: Modify a type — modifiers
text: Type modifiers adjust type enforced given context: const(type): use %:%. Declares constant. optional(type): use typed(). Allows argument missing. maybe(type): use typed(). Allows argument NULL.

title: Modify a type — modifiers
code: const(type)  optional(type)  maybe(type)

title: Modify a type — modifiers
text: type type, e.g. t_int.

title: Modify a type — modifiers
text: modified copy type.

title: Modify a type — modifiers
code: # const() variables can't be assigned to const(t_int) %:% x(1L) x #> [1] 1 try(x <- 2L) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : Can't assign to the constant `x`. #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type.  # optional() arguments may be missing f <- typed(function(x = optional(t_int)) {   if (missing(x)) { "absent" } else { x } }) f() #> [1] "absent" f(1L) #> [1] 1  # maybe() arguments may be `NULL` g <- typed(function(x = maybe(t_int)) x) g(NULL) #> NULL g(1L) #> [1] 1

what: Check an object against a type — obj-type
title: Check an object against a type — obj-type
text: functions used investiage whether object obj type type. obj_is_type(obj, type) returns TRUE obj type type FALSE otherwise. obj_inspect_type(obj, type) prints success failure message type check run obj returns NULL invisibly. obj_assert_type(obj, type) raises <type_error_mistyped_obj> error obj mistyped returns NULL invisibly otherwise.

title: Check an object against a type — obj-type
code: obj_is_type(obj, type)  obj_inspect_type(obj, type, obj_name = rlang::caller_arg(obj))  obj_assert_type(obj, type, obj_name = rlang::caller_arg(obj))

title: Check an object against a type — obj-type
text: obj object check. type type check . obj_name name obj, used messages. Defaults expression passed obj.

title: Check an object against a type — obj-type
text: obj_is_type(): TRUE obj correct type, FALSE otherwise. obj_inspect_type(): NULL invisibly, called side effect. obj_assert_type(): NULL invisibly obj correct type, otherwise raises error.

title: Check an object against a type — obj-type
code: good <- TRUE bad <- NA  # Test whether an object is a boolean obj_is_type(good, t_bool) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(bad, t_bool) #> [1] FALSE  # Print the type tests run on the object obj_inspect_type(good, t_bool) #> Object `good` has the expected type. #> ✔ `good` is a bare <logical>. #> ✔ `good` is size 1. #> ✔ `good` contains no missing values. obj_inspect_type(bad, t_bool) #> Object `bad` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `bad` is a bare <logical>. #> ✔ `bad` is size 1. #> ℹ `bad` must not contain missing elements. #> ✖ `bad` is NA at location `1`.  # Raise an error if the object is not a boolean obj_assert_type(good, t_bool) try(obj_assert_type(bad, t_bool)) #> Error in obj_assert_type(bad, t_bool) : Object `bad` is mistyped. #> ℹ `bad` must not contain missing elements. #> ✖ `bad` is NA at location `1`. #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type.

what: Select an element or attribute to type — on
title: Select an element or attribute to type — on
text: Selectors identify part object use () relation functions same_sized() same_classed(). useful typing elements container type, list. example, following defines coordinate type t_coords, list containing named numeric elements "lat" "lon" size:   () specifies accessor function, either call function name, applied object type checking. accessor call, .x may used placeholder object.   on_elm(index) on_attr(name) convenience selectors common cases (.x[[index]]) (attr(.x, name)).   on_elms(indices) on_attrs(names) multiple selector forms on_elm() on_attr(), useful selecting multiple elements relation:   on_data() selects underlying data object attributes class removed (via unclass()).   on_each() selects elements object, applying type check one individually.
code: t_coords <- t_list |>   has(on_elm("lat"), t_dbl |> bounded(-90, 90)) |>   has(on_elm("lon"), t_dbl |> bounded(-180, 180)) |>   has_relation(same_sized(on_elm("lat"), on_elm("lon"))) # Require an object's length to be between 1 and 10 (inclusive) t_any |> has(on(length(.x)), t_int |> bounded(1L, 10L))  # Require an object's names to contain "x" and "y" # Equivilant to `on(names(.x))` t_any |> has(on(names), t_chr |> contains(c("x", "y"))) has(t_any, on_elm(1L), t_int)     # same as `on(.x[[1L]])` has(t_any, on_attr("dim"), t_int) # same as `on(attr(.x, "dim"))` # These are equivilant t_any |> has_relation(same_sized(on_elms(c(1L, 2L)))) t_any |> has_relation(same_sized(on_elm(1L), on_elm(2L))) # POSIXct datetime vectors store time as a double t_posixct <- t_any |>   classed("POSIXct") |>   has(on_data(), t_dbl) |>   has(on_attr("tzone"), t_chr |> sized(1L)) # Defines a list-of-integers type t_list_of_int <- t_list |> has(on_each(), t_int)

title: Select an element or attribute to type — on
code: on(accessor)  on_attr(name)  on_elm(index)  on_data()  on_each()  on_elms(indices)  on_attrs(attrs)

title: Select an element or attribute to type — on
text: accessor call using .x object placeholder, bare symbol f shorthand f(.x). name on_attr(), single attribute name (character). index on_elm(), single position (integer) name (character). indices on_elms(), positions (integer) names (character). attrs on_attrs(), attribute names (character).

title: Select an element or attribute to type — on
text: selector. functions may used within () relations same_sized() same_classed(). Outside contexts, () functions raise error.

title: Select an element or attribute to type — on
code: t_coords <- t_list |>   has(on(names), t_chr |> setequal_to(c("lat", "lon"))) |>   has(on_elm("lat"), t_dbl |> bounded(-90, 90)) |>   has(on_elm("lon"), t_dbl |> bounded(-180, 180)) |>   has_relation(same_sized(on_elms(c("lat", "lon"))))  good <- list(lat = c(70, 20, -50), lon = c(100, 0, 85)) obj_is_type(good, t_coords) #> [1] TRUE  bad <- list(lat = c(1, 7), lon = 90) obj_inspect_type(bad, t_coords) #> Object `bad` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `bad` is a bare <list>. #> ✔ `names(bad)` is a bare <character>. #> ✔ `names(bad)` is setequal to: `c("lat", "lon")`. #> ✔ `bad[["lat"]]` is a bare <double>. #> ✔ `bad[["lat"]]` is bounded by [-90, 90]. #> ✔ `bad[["lon"]]` is a bare <double>. #> ✔ `bad[["lon"]]` is bounded by [-180, 180]. #> ℹ `bad[["lat"]]` and `bad[["lon"]]` must be the same size. #> ✖ `bad[["lat"]]` is size 2 and `bad[["lon"]]` is size 1.

title: Set between-element type constraints — relations
text: Relations constrain set values must relate one another. relation function's behaviour depends context: Inside has_relation(): constrains selected elements attributes object. Inside typed(): constrains specified function arguments call time. following relations provided: same_sized(): values must share size, checked via vctrs::vec_size(). same_classed(): values must share class. recyclable(): values must size 1 share size. exclusive(): exactly one value must non-NULL (has_relation()) supplied (typed()).

title: Set between-element type constraints — relations
code: same_classed(...)  same_sized(...)  recyclable(...)  exclusive(...)

title: Set between-element type constraints — relations
text: ... Inside has_relation(), one selectors (e.g. on_elm(), on_elms(), on_attr(), ()). Inside typed(), one arguments.

title: Set between-element type constraints — relations
code: # Require elements be the same size t <- t_any |> has_relation(same_sized(on_elm("a"), on_elm("b"))) obj_inspect_type(list(a = 1:3, b = 1:3), t, obj_name = "obj") #> Object `obj` has the expected type. #> ✔ `obj[["a"]]` and `obj[["b"]]` are the same size. obj_inspect_type(list(a = 1:3, b = 1:2), t, obj_name = "obj") #> Object `obj` does not have the expected type. #> ℹ `obj[["a"]]` and `obj[["b"]]` must be the same size. #> ✖ `obj[["a"]]` is size 3 and `obj[["b"]]` is size 2.  # Require elements to share a class t <- t_any |> has_relation(same_classed(on_elm(1L), on_elm(2L))) obj_inspect_type(list(1L, 2L), t) #> Object `list(1L, 2L)` has the expected type. #> ✔ `list(1L, 2L)[[1]]` and `list(1L, 2L)[[2]]` have the same class. obj_inspect_type(list(1L, "a"), t) #> Object `list(1L, "a")` does not have the expected type. #> ℹ `list(1L, "a")[[1]]` and `list(1L, "a")[[2]]` must have the same class. #> ✖ `list(1L, "a")[[1]]` has class <integer> and `list(1L, "a")[[2]]` has class #>   <character>.  # Require elements be size 1 or the same size t <- t_any |> has_relation(recyclable(on_elm("x"), on_elm("y"))) obj_inspect_type(list(x = 1L, y = 1:3), t) #> Object `list(x = 1L, y = 1:3)` has the expected type. #> ✔ `list(x = 1L, y = 1:3)[["x"]]` and `list(x = 1L, y = 1:3)[["y"]]` are #>   recyclable. obj_inspect_type(list(x = 1:2, y = 1:3), t) #> Object `list(x = 1:2, y = 1:3)` does not have the expected type. #> ℹ `list(x = 1:2, y = 1:3)[["x"]]` and `list(x = 1:2, y = 1:3)[["y"]]` must be #>   recyclable. #> ✖ `list(x = 1:2, y = 1:3)[["x"]]` (size 2) and `list(x = 1:2, y = 1:3)[["y"]]` #>   (size 3) have incompatible sizes.  # Require that exactly one element must be non-NULL or supplied t <- t_any |> has_relation(exclusive(on_elm("x"), on_elm("y"))) obj_inspect_type(list(x = 1L, y = NULL), t) #> Object `list(x = 1L, y = NULL)` has the expected type. #> ✔ Exactly one of `list(x = 1L, y = NULL)[["x"]]` and `list(x = 1L, y = #>   NULL)[["y"]]` are non-NULL. obj_inspect_type(list(x = 1L, y = 1L), t) #> Object `list(x = 1L, y = 1L)` does not have the expected type. #> ℹ Exactly one of `list(x = 1L, y = 1L)[["x"]]` and `list(x = 1L, y = #>   1L)[["y"]]` must be non-NULL. #> ✖ `list(x = 1L, y = 1L)[["x"]]` and `list(x = 1L, y = 1L)[["y"]]` are all #>   non-NULL.  f <- typed(   exclusive(x, y),   function(x = optional(t_any), y = optional(t_any)) NULL ) f(x = 1L) #> NULL try(f(x = 1L, y = "a")) #> Error in f(x = 1L, y = "a") :  #>   Exactly one of arguments `x` and `y` must be supplied. #> ✖ `x` and `y` are all supplied.

what: Check that object is identical to a vector — same_as
title: Check that object is identical to a vector — same_as
text: same_as() returns copy type requires object identical values, checked via vctrs::vec_equal().

title: Check that object is identical to a vector — same_as
code: same_as(type, values)

title: Check that object is identical to a vector — same_as
text: type type. values non-empty, non-list vector object must identical .

title: Check that object is identical to a vector — same_as
text: copy type additional constraint elements objects identical values.

title: Check that object is identical to a vector — same_as
code: t_abc <- t_chr |> same_as(c("a", "b", "c")) obj_inspect_type(c("a", "b", "c"), t_abc) #> Object `c("a", "b", "c")` has the expected type. #> ✔ `c("a", "b", "c")` is a bare <character>. #> ✔ `c("a", "b", "c")` is the same as: `c("a", "b", "c")`. obj_inspect_type(c("c", "b", "a"), t_abc) #> Object `c("c", "b", "a")` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `c("c", "b", "a")` is a bare <character>. #> ℹ `c("c", "b", "a")` must be: `c("a", "b", "c")`. #> ✖ `c("c", "b", "a")` differs at locations `c(1, 3)`: #> • Actual: `c("c", "a")` #> • Expected: `c("a", "c")`

what: Check that object is a given set — setequal_to
title: Check that object is a given set — setequal_to
text: setequal_to() returns copy type requires object setequal values, checked via vctrs::vec_in().

title: Check that object is a given set — setequal_to
code: setequal_to(type, values)

title: Check that object is a given set — setequal_to
text: type type. values non-empty, non-list vector values object must setequal .

title: Check that object is a given set — setequal_to
text: copy type additional constraint objects setequal values.

title: Check that object is a given set — setequal_to
code: t_three <- t_int |> setequal_to(1:3) obj_inspect_type(c(1L, 2L, 3L, 1L), t_three) #> Object `c(1L, 2L, 3L, 1L)` has the expected type. #> ✔ `c(1L, 2L, 3L, 1L)` is a bare <integer>. #> ✔ `c(1L, 2L, 3L, 1L)` is setequal to: `c(1, 2, 3)`. obj_inspect_type(10L, t_three) #> Object `10L` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `10L` is a bare <integer>. #> ℹ `10L` must contain exactly: `c(1, 2, 3)`. #> ✖ `10L` is missing 3 elements: `c(1, 2, 3)`. #> ✖ `10L` contains 1 unexpected element: `10`.

title: Check size — sized
text: sized() returns copy type requires objects size size, checked via vctrs::vec_size().
code: obj_is_type(1, t_any |> sized(2L))   # FALSE obj_is_type(1:2, t_any |> sized(2L)) # TRUE

title: Check size — sized
code: sized(type, size)

title: Check size — sized
text: type type. size non-negative count.

title: Check size — sized
text: copy type additional size constraint.

title: Check size — sized
code: t_int2 <- t_int |> sized(2L) obj_inspect_type(10L, t_int2) #> Object `10L` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `10L` is a bare <integer>. #> ✖ `10L` must be size 2, not size 1. obj_inspect_type(1:2, t_int2) #> Object `1:2` has the expected type. #> ✔ `1:2` is a bare <integer>. #> ✔ `1:2` is size 2.

what: Type constructors for lists and data frames — type-constructors
title: Type constructors for lists and data frames — type-constructors
text: functions construct types common R container objects: list_type() creates type named list specific element types. list_of_type() creates type list every element shares type. dataframe_type() creates type data frame specific column types.
code: # A list with an integer "id" and a string "label" t_record <- list_type(id = t_int, label = t_string)  # A list where every element is a double vector t_dbl_list <- list_of_type(t_dbl)  # A data frame with columns "x" (double) and "y" (character) t_df <- dataframe_type(x = t_dbl, y = t_chr)

title: Type constructors for lists and data frames — type-constructors
code: list_type(...)  list_of_type(type)  dataframe_type(...)

title: Type constructors for lists and data frames — type-constructors
text: ... list_type() dataframe_type(), named types element column object. Dots must uniquely named least one dot must supplied. type list_of_type(), type every element list must satisfy.

title: Type constructors for lists and data frames — type-constructors
text: type.

title: Type constructors for lists and data frames — type-constructors
code: t_person <- list_type(name = t_string, age = t_int |> bounded(0L))  good <- list(name = "Alice", age = 30L) bad <- list(name = "Bob", age = -1L) obj_is_type(good, t_person) #> [1] TRUE obj_inspect_type(bad, t_person) #> Object `bad` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `bad` is a bare <list>. #> ✔ `names(bad)` is a bare <character>. #> ✔ `names(bad)` is the same as: `c("name", "age")`. #> ✔ `bad[["name"]]` is a bare <character>. #> ✔ `bad[["name"]]` is size 1. #> ✔ `bad[["name"]]` contains no missing values. #> ℹ `bad[["age"]]` must be equal to or above 0. #> ✖ `bad[["age"]]` is out of bounds at location `1`.  t_scores <- list_of_type(t_dbl |> bounded(0, 100)) good_scores <- list(82.5, 91.0, 74.3) bad_scores <- list(82.5, 110.0) obj_is_type(good_scores, t_scores) #> [1] TRUE obj_inspect_type(bad_scores, t_scores) #> Object `bad_scores` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `bad_scores` is a bare <list>. #> ℹ `bad_scores[[2]]` must be bounded by [0, 100]. #> ✖ `bad_scores[[2]]` is out of bounds at location `1`.  t_coords <- dataframe_type(   lat = t_dbl |> bounded(-90, 90),   lon = t_dbl |> bounded(-180, 180) ) good_df <- data.frame(lat = c(51.5, 40.7), lon = c(-0.1, -74.0)) bad_df <- data.frame(lat = c(51.5, 200.0)) obj_is_type(good_df, t_coords) #> [1] TRUE obj_inspect_type(bad_df, t_coords) #> Object `bad_df` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `bad_df` inherits from class `data.frame`. #> ℹ `names(bad_df)` must be: `c("lat", "lon")`. #> ✖ `names(bad_df)` is missing 1 element: `"lon"`.

what: type: Runtime Type Validation for Functions and Objects — type-package
title: type: Runtime Type Validation for Functions and Objects — type-package
text: Provides lightweight type system 'R'. Types represented composable sets predicates valid object must satisfy. Variables may declared type, checked everytime variable assigned . Additionally, function arguments return values may annotated type, validated time function called.

title: type: Runtime Type Validation for Functions and Objects — type-package
text: Maintainer: Ethan Sansom ethan.sansom29@gmail.com (ORCID) [copyright holder] Authors: Ethan Sansom ethan.sansom29@gmail.com (ORCID) [copyright holder]

what: Test if an object is a type — type-predicates
title: Test if an object is a type — type-predicates
text: Test object type

title: Test if an object is a type — type-predicates
code: is_type(x)  is_type_union(x)

title: Test if an object is a type — type-predicates
text: x object test.

title: Test if an object is a type — type-predicates
text: TRUE x type, FALSE otherwise.

title: Test if an object is a type — type-predicates
code: is_type(10L) #> [1] FALSE is_type(t_int) #> [1] TRUE

title: Declare a type union — type_union
text: type_union() returns type requires object satisfy least one types supplied ....   Nested unions flattened, type_union(type_union(t_int, t_dbl), t_chr) equivalent type_union(t_int, t_dbl, t_chr). Duplicate types silently dropped.
code: # Must be an integer OR a double t_number <- type_union(t_int, t_dbl)  # Must be a string OR NULL t_opt_string <- type_union(t_string, t_null)

title: Declare a type union — type_union
code: type_union(...)

title: Declare a type union — type_union
text: ... Two types combine. least one must supplied.

title: Declare a type union — type_union
text: type accepts objects matching supplied types.

title: Declare a type union — type_union
code: t_index <- type_union(t_int, t_chr) obj_is_type(1L, t_index) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type("a", t_index) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(10.6, t_index) #> [1] FALSE  # Useful for nullable types t_opt_chr <- type_union(t_chr, t_null) obj_is_type(c("a", "b"), t_opt_chr) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(NULL, t_opt_chr) #> [1] TRUE obj_is_type(1L, t_opt_chr) #> [1] FALSE

title: Declare a type-checked function — typed
text: typed() inserts argument (optionally) return type validation function. printed, typed function shows types arguments return value.

title: Declare a type-checked function — typed
code: typed(..., returns = NULL)

title: Declare a type-checked function — typed
text: ... type annotated function definition, optionally accompanied one relation calls (e.g. same_sized()). Exactly one function definition must supplied. returns type function's return value. default returns NULL, meaning return value can type.

title: Declare a type-checked function — typed
text: typed function.

title: Declare a type-checked function — typed
text: typed function's arguments may annotated valid type, e.g. t_int, including types refined additional traits, e.g. sized().   default, annotated function arguments default value. set one, use syntax arg = <type> %:% <default>.   Dots (...) may also type annotated. default, dot checked supplied type. treat dots single argument, e.g. list, use t_dots annotation.   Arguments, exception ..., may modified using optional() maybe(). optional() arguments may unsupplied maybe() arguments may NULL.
code: f <- typed(function(x = t_int, y = t_chr |> sized(1L)) { paste(x, y) }) f(1L, "a")         # ok f(TRUE, "a")       # error, `x` is not an integer f(1L, c("a", "b")) # error, `y` is not a size 1 f <- typed(function(x = t_int %:% 0L) { x }) f()    # 0L f(1L)  # 1L # Each `...` must be an integer f <- typed(function(... = t_int) list(...))  # Exactly 2 dots must be supplied g <- typed(function(... = t_dots |> sized(2L)) list(...)) # `x` is an integer or is unsupplied f <- typed(function(x = optional(t_int)) if (missing(x)) 0L else x)  # `x` is an integer or `NULL` g <- typed(function(x = maybe(t_int)) x)

title: Declare a type-checked function — typed
text: Relations, e.g. same_sized(), declare -argument checks. relation call may placed function definition.
code: # `x` and `y` must be integers of the same size f <- typed(   same_sized(x, y),   function(x = t_int, y = t_int) x + y )

title: Declare a type-checked function — typed
text: Use returns argument enforce type return value. Return types modified using optional() maybe().
code: f <- typed(   function(x = t_bool) if (x) "yes" else "no",   returns = t_chr )

title: Declare a type-checked function — typed
code: any2 <- typed(   function(... = t_lgl, na.rm = t_bool %:% FALSE) {      any(..., na.rm)    },   returns = t_lgl |> sized(1L) ) print(any2) #> <typed> #> function (..., na.rm = FALSE)  #> { #>     any(..., na.rm) #> } #> <environment: 0x55df188dda00> #> Arguments: #> • Each element of `...` is a bare <logical>. #> • `na.rm` is a bare <logical>. #> • `na.rm` is size 1. #> • `na.rm` contains no missing values. #> Returns: #> • `<result>` is a bare <logical>. #> • `<result>` is size 1.  # Correctly typed inputs proceed normally any2(c(TRUE, FALSE), TRUE, na.rm = FALSE) #> [1] TRUE  # Mistyped inputs cause an error try(any2(TRUE, na.rm = "no")) #> Error in any2(TRUE, na.rm = "no") : Argument `na.rm` is mistyped. #> ✖ `na.rm` must be a bare <logical>, not a bare <character>. #> ℹ Run `last_type()` to get the expected type.

title: Check for duplicate values — unduplicated
text: unduplicated() returns copy type requires objects contain duplicate values, checked via vctrs::vec_duplicate_any().

title: Check for duplicate values — unduplicated
code: unduplicated(type)

title: Check for duplicate values — unduplicated
text: type type.

title: Check for duplicate values — unduplicated
text: copy type additional non-duplicate constraint.

title: Check for duplicate values — unduplicated
code: t_ids <- t_chr |> unduplicated() obj_inspect_type(c("a1", "a2"), t_ids) #> Object `c("a1", "a2")` has the expected type. #> ✔ `c("a1", "a2")` is a bare <character>. #> ✔ `c("a1", "a2")` contains no duplicated values. obj_inspect_type(c("a1", "b1", "b1"), t_ids) #> Object `c("a1", "b1", "b1")` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `c("a1", "b1", "b1")` is a bare <character>. #> ℹ `c("a1", "b1", "b1")` must not contain duplicate elements. #> ✖ `c("a1", "b1", "b1")` contains duplicate elements at locations `c(2, 3)`.

what: Remove argument and return type validation from a typed function — untyped
title: Remove argument and return type validation from a typed function — untyped
text: untyped() removes argument return types typed function declared via typed(). fun non-typed function, fun returned .

title: Remove argument and return type validation from a typed function — untyped
code: untyped(fun)

title: Remove argument and return type validation from a typed function — untyped
text: fun function untype.

title: Remove argument and return type validation from a typed function — untyped
text: function, without typed() arguments return values.

title: Remove argument and return type validation from a typed function — untyped
code: foo <- typed(function(x = t_int) { x }) print(foo) #> <typed> #> function (x)  #> { #>     x #> } #> <environment: 0x55df1c4a6728> #> Arguments: #> • `x` is a bare <integer>. #> Returns: #> • `<result>` is an R object. print(untyped(foo)) #> function (x)  #> { #>     x #> } #> <environment: 0x55df1c4a6728>

what: Check that object is a subset — within
title: Check that object is a subset — within
text: within() returns copy type requires every element object within values, checked via vctrs::vec_in().

title: Check that object is a subset — within
code: within(type, values)

title: Check that object is a subset — within
text: type type. values non-empty, non-list vector values elements object must within.

title: Check that object is a subset — within
text: copy type additional constraint objects within values.

title: Check that object is a subset — within
code: t_weekend <- t_chr |> within(c("Sat", "Sun")) obj_inspect_type("Sat", t_weekend) #> Object `"Sat"` has the expected type. #> ✔ `"Sat"` is a bare <character>. #> ✔ `"Sat"` contains only values from: `c("Sat", "Sun")`. obj_inspect_type(c("A", "B"), t_weekend) #> Object `c("A", "B")` does not have the expected type. #> ✔ `c("A", "B")` is a bare <character>. #> ℹ `c("A", "B")` may only contain values from: `c("Sat", "Sun")`. #> ✖ `c("A", "B")` contains 2 unexpected elements: `c("A", "B")`.
